_meta.source: Synthesized from 44 per-notebook technique analyses of NirDiamant/RAG_Techniques plus _repo-overview.json
_meta.relation_semantics: Edges are directed source -> target. composes_with: source and target stack in one pipeline (symmetric, may appear on either or both sides). alternative_to: source and target solve the same problem differently (symmetric). refines: source is an improved/extended/drop-in-upgraded version of target. prerequisite_of: source must be in place before target is useful (edge lives on the prerequisite). evaluated_by: target is an evaluation harness that measures source's quality.
normalizations: simple_rag_with_llamaindex stage changed indexing -> retrieval to match simple_rag (framework variants share the base technique's stage)
context_enrichment_window_around_chunk_with_llamaindex stage changed chunking -> post-retrieval to match context_enrichment_window_around_chunk

Dropped dangling relation targets with no notebook/analysis: multi_faceted_filtering (README-only conceptual entry referenced by 7 analyses), dsRAG (external library), Controllable-RAG-Agent (external repo), 'evaluation-with-ragas' (RAGAS is internal to end-2-end_rag_evaluation), 'metadata_filtering-style approaches' (json_rag)

Normalized analyst slug spellings: HyDe_Hypothetical_Document_Embedding -> hyde_hypothetical_document_embedding, HyPE_Hypothetical_Prompt_Embeddings -> hype_hypothetical_prompt_embeddings, Microsoft_GraphRag/microsoft-graphrag -> microsoft_graphrag, grouse -> evaluation_grouse, deep_evaluation/deepeval -> evaluation_deep_eval, hyphenated slugs (self-rag, fusion-retrieval, ...) -> underscored canonical slugs
Repaired mangled relation entry in graph_rag_local_attribution ('multi_model_rag_with_captioning-or-plain vector RAG (simple_rag)') -> simple_rag alternative_to

stages.name: Chunking
stages.description: How documents are split into retrieval units before anything is embedded: fixed-size tuning, semantic boundaries, atomic propositions, and per-chunk context headers.

stages.name: Indexing & Index Enrichment
stages.description: How the searchable index is built and enriched beyond plain chunk embeddings: synthetic-question embeddings, summary tiers, knowledge graphs, structured-data ingestion, and multimodal captioning.

stages.name: Query Transformation
stages.description: Reshaping the user's query before search: rewriting, step-back, decomposition, hypothetical answer documents, and memory-grounded surrogate queries.

stages.name: Retrieval
stages.description: The search execution itself: dense baseline, hybrid dense+sparse fusion, diversity-aware selection, query-type routing, graph traversal, and vision-based page retrieval.

stages.name: Post-Retrieval Processing
stages.description: What happens to candidates between retriever and generator: reranking, compression, neighbor-window expansion, segment reconstruction, relevance gating, explanation, and feedback-driven re-scoring.

stages.name: Orchestration & Architecture
stages.description: Pipeline-level control flow and end-to-end architectures: self-reflective loops, corrective routing with fallbacks, managed agentic platforms, and fully-local deployments.

stages.name: Evaluation
stages.description: Measuring RAG quality offline: LLM-as-judge metrics for correctness/faithfulness/relevancy, grounded-QA frameworks, reference-free scoring, and meta-evaluation of the judges themselves.

failure_modes.name: No grounded QA baseline
failure_modes.description: There is no retrieval pipeline at all yet: the LLM cannot answer questions about private documents, and the documents are too long to paste into the context window.
example_phrasings: The model hallucinates or says 'I don't know' about my own PDFs
My documents don't fit in the context window
I need a working chat-with-my-docs starting point

failure_modes.name: Query-document vocabulary and style mismatch
failure_modes.description: Users phrase queries differently from the corpus (short questions vs long declarative prose, synonyms, jargon), so embedding similarity ranks the truly relevant passage below superficially similar ones.
example_phrasings: Search misses the right passage when the question uses different words than the document
My users ask short questions but my docs are dense formal prose
The chunk that answers the question exists but never comes back in top-k

failure_modes.name: Exact terms, IDs, and rare tokens missed
failure_modes.description: Dense embeddings smooth over rare words, product codes, acronyms, and identifiers, so queries for exact strings return only vaguely related chunks.
example_phrasings: Searching for a specific error code or part number returns unrelated text
Embeddings can't find the doc that literally contains my search term
Acronyms and domain jargon retrieve the wrong passages

failure_modes.name: Retrieved chunk lacks surrounding context
failure_modes.description: The matched chunk is relevant but cut off mid-idea or stripped of the neighboring sentences/sections it depends on, so the generator answers from an incomplete fragment.
example_phrasings: Retrieved chunks are cut off mid-sentence and the answer is incomplete
The fact I need is in the sentence right after the chunk that matched
Answers read as disjointed because the model only sees isolated snippets

failure_modes.name: Irrelevant chunks pollute the context window
failure_modes.description: Top-k similarity returns off-topic or marginally relevant chunks that dilute the signal, distract the generator, and crowd out the passages that actually answer the question.
example_phrasings: The answer is buried among unrelated sentences in what got retrieved
Adding more retrieved chunks makes answers worse, not better
The model gets derailed by off-topic text that sat in the same chunk

failure_modes.name: Right passage retrieved but ranked too low
failure_modes.description: The correct chunk is in the candidate pool but sits below the k cutoff passed to the generator, because raw embedding similarity cannot judge nuanced relevance.
example_phrasings: The correct passage is at rank 8-20 and gets cut before generation
Chunks that mention my keywords outrank the one that actually answers
I want high recall retrieval but only send a few precise chunks to the LLM

failure_modes.name: Redundant or one-sided results
failure_modes.description: Top-k results are near-duplicates of each other or all cover a single aspect/viewpoint, so raising k adds no new information and multi-faceted questions get one-sided answers.
example_phrasings: My top results all repeat the same passage
The corpus has duplicated content and retrieval keeps surfacing the copies
Questions about debates return documents that all take the same side

failure_modes.name: Multi-hop questions fail
failure_modes.description: Answering requires chaining facts scattered across different chunks or documents; flat top-k retrieval fetches each fragment independently and never connects them.
example_phrasings: Questions that need facts from several places connected together get wrong answers
How-are-X-and-Y-related questions fail completely
No single chunk contains the full chain of reasoning

failure_modes.name: Corpus-level and thematic questions unanswerable
failure_modes.description: Questions about themes, overall trends, summaries, or aggregates over the whole corpus cannot be answered from any single retrieved chunk.
example_phrasings: 'What are the main themes of this report?' returns shallow answers
Questions needing a global picture of a 600-page book fail
The system never aggregates or counts across the corpus

failure_modes.name: Multi-part questions only partially answered
failure_modes.description: A single query embedding cannot cover every facet of a compound question, so retrieval collapses onto one aspect and the rest go unanswered.
example_phrasings: Complex multi-part questions get only partially answered
Compare/contrast questions pull chunks about only one side
Broad questions get answered from a single narrow angle

failure_modes.name: Vague, underspecified, or conversational queries
failure_modes.description: The user's query is too vague, high-level, or conversationally phrased to be a good search key; nothing in the corpus literally matches its wording or intent.
example_phrasings: Vague questions return generic off-topic chunks
Users don't know how to phrase questions in the corpus's terms
Multi-turn follow-ups lose the context of the conversation

failure_modes.name: One retrieval strategy doesn't fit all query types
failure_modes.description: The workload mixes factual lookups, analytical questions, opinions, and keyword queries; tuning retrieval for one type degrades the others.
example_phrasings: Retrieval works for fact lookups but fails on explain/compare questions
Some queries need exact matching, others need semantic understanding
Quality is inconsistent across different kinds of questions

failure_modes.name: Answers not grounded in retrieved evidence
failure_modes.description: Generated answers contain claims the retrieved documents do not support: the model mixes parametric knowledge with evidence, or answers confidently from irrelevant context.
example_phrasings: Answers sound plausible but aren't supported by the sources
The model answers from its own knowledge instead of my documents
Hallucinated facts slip through with no automated detection

failure_modes.name: Cannot trace answers to sources
failure_modes.description: Users, auditors, or regulators cannot verify which exact passage supports each claim; citations are missing, wrong, or too coarse (whole-document instead of sentence-level).
example_phrasings: I can't tell which sentence in my corpus supports this claim
Users don't trust answers because there's no visible evidence
The answer cites source [1] but I don't know if it really supports the claim

failure_modes.name: Retrieval is a black box
failure_modes.description: Nobody can explain why the retriever returned what it did; debugging poor retrieval is guesswork because similarity scores carry no rationale.
example_phrasings: I can't tell why these chunks were retrieved for my query
Debugging bad retrieval is pure guesswork
I need a visible trace of how the system got from query to evidence

failure_modes.name: Out-of-scope questions, stale or conflicting knowledge
failure_modes.description: The knowledge base doesn't cover the question, is outdated, or contains conflicting versions of the truth - and the system forces an answer instead of falling back, declining, or resolving the conflict.
example_phrasings: Questions outside my document collection get made-up answers instead of a fallback
My knowledge base is outdated so answers about recent facts are stale
Old and new versions of documents conflict and the retriever can't be told which to trust

failure_modes.name: Token cost, latency, or context budget blown
failure_modes.description: The pipeline spends too much per query: oversized contexts inflate cost and latency, retrieval fires even when unneeded, or per-query LLM calls make a good technique too slow.
example_phrasings: Answers are slow because prompts are stuffed with oversized context
Query rewriting improved quality but made every query slower and more expensive
The system retrieves for every query, even chit-chat, adding noise and cost

failure_modes.name: Chunk size or boundaries wrong for the corpus
failure_modes.description: Fixed-size splitting cuts mid-idea, blends unrelated topics into one embedding, or produces chunks too small/large for the workload - and no single setting fits heterogeneous documents.
example_phrasings: Chunks blend two topics so embeddings are muddy
Chunk size was chosen by gut feeling with no evidence it fits
No chunk size works for both factoid lookups and section-level questions

failure_modes.name: Chunks lose document and section identity
failure_modes.description: After splitting, chunks refer to their subject with pronouns or implicit references and don't encode which document/section they came from, so retrieval pulls similar-sounding chunks from the wrong document and the generator misreads them.
example_phrasings: A chunk about my topic never names the entity so search skips it
Similar chunks from the wrong document pollute results in a multi-document corpus
Retrieved text says 'the company' and nobody knows which company

failure_modes.name: Structured and tabular data doesn't fit document RAG
failure_modes.description: The data is CSV rows, JSON records, or spreadsheets; naive text chunking splits records apart, drops column names, and mixes fields from different records.
example_phrasings: My data is spreadsheets/JSON, not documents, and chunking mangles it
Retrieved values come back without their column names
Lookups about a specific record return pieces of several rows mixed together

failure_modes.name: Answers live in figures, tables, and charts
failure_modes.description: Critical content is visual - charts, diagrams, complex tables, scanned pages - which text extraction mangles or drops entirely, so it is invisible to the retriever.
example_phrasings: Questions about a chart or table in a PDF get wrong or no answers
PDF text extraction ignores the images where the answer lives
My OCR/parsing pipeline keeps breaking on complex layouts

failure_modes.name: Data cannot leave local infrastructure
failure_modes.description: Confidentiality, compliance, or cost rules forbid sending documents to cloud LLM/embedding APIs; the pipeline must run fully local or offline.
example_phrasings: My documents are confidential and must not leave our servers
Per-token API billing makes my workload too expensive
I need RAG to work offline

failure_modes.name: No way to measure quality or catch regressions
failure_modes.description: There is no scoring harness: quality is judged by eyeballing outputs, changes to chunking/retrieval/prompts have no regression signal, and specific failure dimensions (completeness, faithfulness, relevancy) are invisible.
example_phrasings: I changed my retriever and can't tell if it got better or worse
I have no ground-truth answers so I can't measure anything
One opaque quality score hides which dimension is failing

failure_modes.name: The LLM judge itself is unreliable
failure_modes.description: Evaluation relies on an LLM judge whose agreement with humans or a strong reference judge is unknown; cheap judges produce inconsistent, uncalibrated scores.
example_phrasings: I use a cheap LLM as judge but have no idea if its grades are right
My one-shot judge prompt disagrees with human labels, especially on negatives
My evaluator is itself unevaluated

failure_modes.name: System never learns from user feedback
failure_modes.description: The pipeline keeps making the same mistakes: user ratings and past good answers are never folded back into retrieval or ranking.
example_phrasings: The system gives the same bad answer even after users flag it
Retrieval never improves no matter how much feedback users give
Documents users consistently rate unhelpful keep ranking first

categories.notes: The README's 'foundational' grab-bag spans four of our stages; reliable_rag is foundational per the README but functionally a post-retrieval validation layer.

README itself is inconsistent here: its summary table files HyPE under Query Enhancement while its detailed list files it under Context Enrichment. We stage HyPE as indexing because its LLM cost is paid at index time.
The README also lists 'Multi-faceted Filtering' here, which has NO notebook in the repo (broken link); it is excluded from this ontology and all analyst references to it were dropped.
define_evaluation_metrics exists in the repo but is absent from the README.
The 'Sophisticated Controllable Agent' is an external repo (NirDiamant/Controllable-RAG-Agent) with no notebook here; excluded.
Present in the repo but undocumented in the README.
